Don't expect the usual "Avenger/Marvel" quality I had high hopes for Black Widow to be a movie on the same level - script, acting, action, grandeur - as the early Marvel movies. (Iron Man, Avengers, Thor, Captain America etc) Instead we got a movie that felt like it had zero connection to the Marvel universe at all, or like it was made by people who hadn't seen any of the previous Marvel movies, let alone worked in any of the productions?!  The opening plot was exciting for the first 10 minutes but then it fell flat, and never recovered imho, and halfway through I was convinced Scarlet Johansson was only there to create enough buzz for the others to make a spinoff...  I don't know anything about the director Cate Shortland, nor do I want to find out what other movies she's made in the past because this was so uninspiring. Really poor acting - or directing, whatever's at fault?  Me and my kids have watched all of the Marvel movies up to the final Avengers time and time and time again - which is a true testament to great movies, but Black Widow is a movie I'll never watch again, which is a shame because I really love Scarlet Johansson when she's really into a role! This was a real "meh" moment...  I'd hoped Rachel Weisz and Olga Kurylenko would have great parts and really lift the movie but one of them seemed sleeping and the other only showed her face twice - and only had one tiny line... The only one at least trying to deliver was Florence Pugh - which is obviously set to return in a spinoff.  I'm putting this Marvel film in the same bucket as most of the newer ones, you know the extra bucket for milking that cow dry to the bone, hoping the connection to the original Avenger movies will be enough to print more money, but I'm getting more and more fed up with how these new Marvel movies are made and gone are the good unique superhero movies, replaced with some cookie cutter - often feminist - replacements, and not good ones either.  Your mileage might vary and I've seen reviews ranging from nil to max so please make up your own mind about Black Widow, but it's a Marvel movie I'd gladly had missed despite loving Scarlet Johansson as an actor because she can be really great with a good script and a good director!  I know Jon Favreau is super busy and can't make everything himself, but if Marvel is going to continue making awesome movies we need him behind the reigns, or Taika Waititi for doing comedy variants, which I gather this one tried to be and failed so hard...  P.s. The spoken Russian in the movie is abysmal, despite them supposedly being Russians... I mean couldn't they have spent a weekend with a speech coach teaching them their lines correctly?! My Russian is far from great but enough to make it feel like a comedy, not an action flick.

I Dislike Time Travel Just in case some of you are questioning my 7 out of 10 rating, here is how I usually like to break down how my "stars" are assigned, with my review of each "star."  1-Costumes. Disclaimer: I am very biased on this section, which is maybe why I list it first. I used to work in Entertainment Costuming for eight years, and because we were continually dumped on and forgotten, I pay extra attention to not only the quality and detail of costume pieces, but also take time to think about what it may take to develop and make these costumes.  In case you didn't know, there are kind of *a lot* of lead roles being played in this film, which means these creatives are working at least twice as hard as normal. And honestly, just seeing the trailer shot of the team in their Time Travel Team Suits got me super excited. It's evident that lots of time for development and attention to detail went into just these, and we already know ever version of each heroes costume has been exquisitely done since 2008, so I actually consider two stars for this category. YAY FOR COSTUMES.  2-Do I Care About the Characters? Obviously. Marvel Studios has strung me along and taken money after I've thrown it at them repeatedly, so YES. STAR FOR THIS BECAUSE I CARED BEFORE THE MOVIE EVEN STARTED. I didn't even pick up a comic book before Iron Man released in theatres.  3-Performances (Quality of). This is difficult for me, because this is a very large ensemble cast. Yes, it was mostly concentrated on the main team of surviving eight, but that is still a LOT of lead time compared to most films that have a lead of one to two. I'm not trying to explain this to lead up to say that I believe the performance qualities were poor; not at all. Of course they are good. I love each and everyone of these actors and actresses. But, did they move me to tears and extra feels? No. (Don't @ me on this, I can't help that I didn't cry!) Can I think of any one moment where I was like, WOW I FEEL THAT AND RELATE AND WOW. It just didn't happen, and that's okay. The Russo Brothers did SO GREAT balancing all of the talent. No star for this, though.  4-Was it Cinematic? YES. Star.  5-Music. Honestly, I never noticed the music in this film unless it was the Avengers Team them, so no star for this.  6-Story. STAR. Again; 21 movie arch and had me since 2008. SO MUCH STORY. Come to think of it, TWO STARS. GOOD JOB, MARVEL.  7-Feels? Yes, I felt feels, but it goes back to the performance thing. I can't really think of moments of peak emotion with the exception of when they cut to Cap slinging Thor's hammer around. So, no star.  8-Originality. This was also difficult for me, as these films are based off of the creations of Stan Lee and Jack Kirby, so technically they aren't really original. And with the exception of the fact that Marvel created these interconnected films over 11 years, the first time anything like this has been done, there is not really anything original in any of these films, so I did not give this category a star. Also, even though it was evident some sort of time travel would be happening in order to save the snapped/dusted, I was not satisfied with how it played out. I've seen it twice and have so many questions that I feel aren't going to be answered. And if they do plan on answering them, they have two shots to do so: Far From Home and the spinoffs they will be putting onto their new Disney streaming service in November. Also, they even joked in their own film about all of the time travel films that have been done already. So, not so original. *shrugs*  9-Over all entertainment. STAR. I WILL LIKELY WATCH IT AGAIN.  10-Will I watch it again? SEE 9. STAR.

One of the better of its kind I appreciate the fact that this movie took its time to build some solid relation between the characters, even when it doesn't really mean anything to the story but just like every superhero movie the story is not really interesting and predictable.

Amazing The Avengers, a superhero symphony that had me fist-pumping like a Marvel fanatic. The ensemble cast, from Robert Downey Jr.'s snarky Tony Stark to Scarlett Johansson's kick-ass Black Widow, is a powerhouse of charisma and chemistry, each hero shining in their own right. The film editing is a whirlwind of action, seamlessly interweaving larger-than-life battles with character-driven moments that give the narrative heart. Strengths? The seamless blending of individual hero stories into a cohesive team dynamic, a superhero jigsaw puzzle that fits together flawlessly. Weaknesses? Occasionally, the plot might hover on the edge of over-the-top, but hey, it's a superhero extravaganza, and we love it that way. The music, Alan Silvestri's score, is the pulse of heroism, swelling with each iconic entrance and saving the world crescendo. Cinematography captures the grandeur of the Marvel universe, from the soaring heights of Stark Tower to the depths of Loki's mischief. The message? A celebration of teamwork, diversity, and the idea that even the most disparate heroes can come together for the greater good. 8/10 is a superhero salute to its exhilarating entertainment, a film that not only assembles Earth's mightiest heroes but also assembles a solid spot in the pantheon of superhero epics.

Okay. But nothing more... So, supposedly the best superhero movie of 2025? I don't think so. Too much illogical action, too often cheesy dialogue, too few comprehensible motives. I could go on forever. Character development is virtually nonexistent. Motives aren't questioned, let alone explained. You feel like a house cat who's just bought some random food from the hardware store and is now told "eat or die." Not bad action, and the CGI was good, too. But a film that can be described as okay at best.

Enjoyable and a Worthy Marvel Movie Doesn't matter to everyone who says it's bad. Captain Marvel is a worthy and enjoyable movie about introducing Captain Marvel prior to Avengers: Endgame. Not that I hate everyone who says it's bad but if you think Captain Marvel's plot is unsatisfying, direct the movie on your own then.

Subatomic space opera First episode in the fifth phase of the MCU's Multiverse Saga, and thirty-first film in the Marvel Cinematic Universe, 'Ant-Man and the Wasp: Quantumania' is a subatomic space opera that continues the Ant-Man franchise with great imagination and efficiency.  Its stellar cast creates a large galactic family with actresses and actors of the new generation, as well as Hollywood legends such as Bill Murray, Michael Douglas and Michelle Pfeiffer.  Dimensions, creatures, realms, settings and universes make the most of the infinite possibilities of the multiverse, whose only limits are constituted by the imagination of their creators.

Still not substantial enough to convert those who aren't fans of the MCU. But fair enough it is one of the best Marvel films. For those who have struggled to get into the MCU because they sacrifice depth, story, human interest, in exchange for effects, gimmicks, and escapism, this is sadly still not enough to convert you. However, this is one of the better Marvel films and is fairly rated and considered as such. There was just enough of Â£ dimensional character development and believable acting and storyline to engage with it, at least for the first half. It does descend into mindless action for a section of the film. But I just about held on to the end. Props for the quality soundtrack too.
I loved it But cuz I looked at it from a long time ago so I don't really remember things about it

It should have been a Disney+ Series I think the only issue with this film is that it's a film.  This should have been a series where each episode goes through the nuances of the Eternals, their relationships, and their journey over time. It does feel that when the scene was getting nice and powerful we have to cut to the next scene because it's a movie and we have limited run time.  Considering it as a single movie, Chloe Zhao does a great job is clamoring the whole new lore into the MCU with perfect casting and unique characters. The Story was crisp and I never really felt bored. Yes these stories deserve time and they need to be developed and I feel a series would be great. A fun and fresh space adventure "Guardians of the Galaxy," directed by James Gunn, is a refreshing addition to the Marvel Cinematic Universe, blending humor, action, and a quirky ensemble of characters. The film follows Peter Quill (Chris Pratt), who is abducted from Earth as a child and grows up to become the space-faring rogue Star-Lord. He forms an unlikely alliance with a group of misfits: the fierce Gamora (Zoe Saldana), the literal Drax the Destroyer (Dave Bautista), the wisecracking Rocket Raccoon (voiced by Bradley Cooper), and the lovable tree-like Groot (voiced by Vin Diesel).  The movie's charm lies in its offbeat humor and the chemistry among its main characters. The soundtrack, filled with classic '70s and '80s hits, adds a nostalgic and energetic vibe that complements the film's tone. The visual effects are stunning, bringing the vibrant and diverse galaxy to life.  While the plot revolves around a familiar battle to save the universe from a powerful villain, it's the character development and their camaraderie that make the film stand out. Each member of the Guardians brings their own backstory and personality, creating a dynamic that is both entertaining and heartfelt.  "Guardians of the Galaxy" successfully blends action, comedy, and heart, making it an enjoyable and unique entry in the superhero genre.

A Hilarious yet Flawed Union - Deadpool & Wolverine Review The highly anticipated pairing of Deadpool and Wolverine has finally hit the screens, and while it delivers an abundance of laughter and action, it falls short of being a truly exceptional film due to some pacing and scripting issues.  One of the standout aspects of this movie is the return of Hugh Jackman as Wolverine, who slips back into the iconic role with ease. His presence is a welcome addition to the film, and his chemistry with Ryan Reynolds' Deadpool is undeniable. Reynolds, too, shines as the Merc with a Mouth, bringing his signature wit and irreverence to the role.  The opening scene sets the tone for the film, showcasing an impressive action sequence that seamlessly blends humor and intensity. This tone is maintained throughout the movie, with plenty of laugh-out-loud moments that will leave audiences entertained. However, some of these moments feel stretched or overly reliant on Reynolds' quick wit, which can become exhausting at times.  One of the primary issues with the film is its scripting. While the dialogue is often hilarious, some of Ryan Reynolds' lines feel too heavy-handed and could have been shortened or edited down for better flow. This is particularly noticeable in the film's second act, where the pacing begins to drag due to an overabundance of exposition and meandering subplots.  Despite these flaws, the film's strengths lie in its ability to balance humor and heart. The dynamic between Deadpool and Wolverine is genuinely engaging, and their banter is some of the film's highlights. The action sequences are also well-choreographed and visually stunning, making for an immersive viewing experience.  In conclusion, while Deadpool & Wolverine is an enjoyable film with plenty of laughs and exciting moments, it falls short of being a truly exceptional movie due to some scripting and pacing issues. With some tighter editing and more concise dialogue, this film could have reached its full potential. Nonetheless, fans of both characters will find plenty to love, and the chemistry between Jackman and Reynolds makes for a compelling watch.  Rating: 7/10  Recommendation: If you're a fan of either Deadpool or Wolverine, or enjoy action-comedies with a healthy dose of irreverence, this film is definitely worth watching. However, viewers seeking a more streamlined narrative may find themselves slightly disappointed.

Enjoyable Action Romp This movie improves on the last Thor film. It's nicely paced and has some excellent action scenes. In its eagerness to make clear to the audience that this is going to be a big action movie, the early part has a lot of chaotic noise to it, which is perhaps why the first scene I really liked was a peaceful one in an abandoned building.   Once the movie caught me interest I stayed caught. The story is coherent without any instantly noticeable plot holes, which is about as much as you can hope for from the typical superhero movie, and there are some nice surprises and some good WOAH moments. Performances were good.  It's certainly formulaic, but it uses that formula well.

Boring Action, Flat Storyline and Forced Drama. Black Widow is completely forgettable. Not without it's merits Black Widow quickly devolves into Marvel formula checklists.  As many people are saying Florence Pugh is the highlight of the film though that can be said for every movie she stars in. Not sure if it was intended to have Yelena be the most interesting character instead of Natasha? The chemistry with between Pugh and Johansson is decent enough, and the initial fight between widow and task master was cool.  The family drama is forced. The dinner table scene moves from flat comedy to drama in order to create artificial conflict with the main cast.  However Task Master eventually takes a back seat to the forced family drama and the reveal at the end is predictable. The action itself is one of the most disappointing aspects of the film. None of it was exciting and it's been done better in other marvel movies. Every fight is quick cuts to hide the stunt-double. All of these A list actors take big money but don't put in the effort to learn choreography so we can understand what the heck is going on in a fight. The constant reminder I'm watching a stunt double took me out of every fight in the movie. When the Marvel 3rd act CGI battle happens Natasha turns full CGI and it's boring. All the action is dull and lifeless. Another problem with the action is how slick and boring the style of the movie is. There is no grit in Black Widow. Marvel and Disney are too afraid to take any chances with a tone or style of the movie. Instead of making this solo film something suspense filled and grounded we have a marvel movie we have already seen a million times before. A missed opportunity to make a grounded superhero feel real. Sort of like what Nolan did with his Batman trilogy.  Eventually everything ends in Black Widow after the overlong final battle and everyone is super happy. Natasha changes her hair color and everyone can be happy leaving the theatre. Yawn! Black Widow is pure formula. Never taking any chances to deviate from the path set by ten years of previous marvel films. Everything is so convenient and tidy that I want to roll my eyes. Black Widow never made me gasp in excitement, tremble with suspense or tear up because I become so invested in the characters. Comfort food is what Black Widow is and if that is your thing you're most likely very easy to please and right dumb reviews like the Top review for this movie, or your as sad as anyone who looks at reviews on IMDB and dislikes them because they have differing opinions than you. If you fit into either of those categories I'm sure Black Widow is the movie for you. High production values does not equal substance. You love Black Widow but Hate F9? What is the difference here?

A Fun but Uneven Marvel Mashup "Deadpool & Wolverine" delivers a mix of high-energy action and crude humor that feels both like a love letter to fans and a bit of a chaotic mess.  Ryan Reynolds and Hugh Jackman's chemistry is the glue that holds this film together. Their banter and dynamic are genuinely entertaining, providing some of the movie's best moments. Cameos are plentiful and well-handled, giving fans of the Fox Marvel Universe a delightful send-off, with characters integrated into the plot more than just for show. The action scenes, particularly the car fight, are executed with flair, making for some of the most visually satisfying sequences in recent MCU films.  The plot feels like an afterthought, overshadowed by the barrage of cameos and humor. There's a thin narrative thread that seems to exist just to string together the next gag or surprise guest. Humor can be hit or miss. While there's plenty of laughter to be had, the relentless cursing and juvenile gags might wear thin for some viewers, feeling more like an attempt to prove its R-rating rather than enhancing the story. The movie sometimes feels too self-aware, almost to its detriment, with fourth-wall breaks and meta-commentary that can pull you out of the experience rather than enhancing it.  "Deadpool & Wolverine" is a fun, if somewhat overwhelming, ride. It's a celebration of the characters and the universe they inhabit, but it doesn't push the boundaries of storytelling or character development much beyond what we've seen in previous Deadpool films. If you're here for the laughs, action, and fan service, you won't be disappointed, but if you're looking for depth or a compelling narrative, you might leave feeling a bit unsatisfied. A good time at the movies, no alcohol required, but it's not the MCU savior some might have hoped for.

Did I see a different movie to most people? *** SPOILERS ***  I just don't get the rave reviews for Infinity War. Yes, the action is great. Yes, the special effects are great. Yes, there's a pretty good balance between most of the different characters; and seeing some of our favourite characters meet up with some of our other favourites was also great. I just can't understand people raving about the ending...  *** SPOILER REMINDER ***  Given that we know there are a few more films in the pipeline- Spidey and Guardians to name just two, to have those main characters killed off was pointless because we know that they are going to have to be resurrected in Infinity War 2 (before the new stand alone character films are released). However they do this, (time reversal or diving into the soul stone or whatever), it simply means that characters are going to be resurrected. For all we know they all will be, and Thanos will seem like an insignificant blip on the radar because everything he did possibly gets undone. It was so jarring because I didn't believe it for a second that the 'deaths' were real (ie. Black Panther, Spidey and the Guardians). I knew they had to be resurrected. It just killed the tension, it killed the atmosphere- I mean why should I cry for Parker when I KNOW he's coming back? By the time the end credits stinger rolled around and more deaths occurred, I was snarling contemptuously at the screen. I was so bitterly disappointed. It honestly (and I mean this wholeheartedly) feels like the last Twilight film- there's a super cool battle that essentially is just a dream- it never really happened. It's the same plot device and I am stunned to hear people saying that the end of A:IW was heart wrenching. How gullible do people have to be? Just so you know, I'm the kind of film viewer who doesn't critique everything while I'm watching a film. I like to just enjoy the experience, absorbed in the events unfolding before me. A:IW just didn't work at all, the writers bit off way more than we could chew! I really want to rate it 2 stars because I'm so disappointed by the ending, but as I mentioned, there were a lot of really good things about the film.

Magnificent return to form for Marvel This has all the well-done action, fights, physical struggles etc one hopes for in a superhero movie, but where it really shines is in its emotional core.  Viewers familiar with the comics will not encounter any huge surprises in terms of plot, though there's two good minor ones: one early, one late. Viewers with no knowledge of the characters except what they've already seen in the MCU are in for a few more twists. But the plot, action, explosions, chases, punching, etc, while all well done, are just gravy compared to the resonantly and movingly handled themes.  There are stand-out performances from Pugh and Pullman, but it's a strong ensemble performance all round.  This is a must-see for MCU fans, and I'd even tentatively recommend it to people who have never seen a Marvel movie before (so long as they have at least an open mind about the concept of "superhero").

Unpacking the Brilliance of Iron Man 3 Iron Man 3, directed by Shane Black, is a 2013 superhero thriller that delves into the complexities of Tony Stark's character, exposing his vulnerabilities and insecurities. This film is a game-changer in the Marvel Cinematic Universe, offering a fresh and introspective perspective on the iconic superhero.  The movie takes place after the events of The Avengers, where Tony Stark is struggling to come to terms with the trauma he experienced during the alien invasion. As he tries to find a way to cope with his anxiety and PTSD, a new threat emerges in the form of the Mandarin, a terrorist who is using advanced technology to wreak havoc on the world.  Robert Downey Jr. Shines as Tony Stark/Iron Man, bringing his signature wit and charm to the role. However, this time around, we see a more vulnerable side of Tony, as he grapples with his inner demons. The supporting cast, including Gwyneth Paltrow, Don Cheadle, and Guy Pearce, deliver solid performances that add depth to the story.  The action sequences in Iron Man 3 are some of the most impressive in the Marvel Cinematic Universe. The movie's visuals are stunning, with the Iron Man suits looking more sleek and advanced than ever before. The film's climax, which features an epic battle between Iron Man and the Mandarin's minions, is both thrilling and visually stunning.  One of the standout aspects of Iron Man 3 is its exploration of themes such as trauma, vulnerability, and the psychological effects of being a superhero. The movie shows Tony Stark's struggles with anxiety and PTSD, making him a more relatable and human character. The film also touches on the theme of identity, as Tony Stark grapples with the question of who he is without the Iron Man suit.  Iron Man 3 is a thought-provoking and visually stunning film that is a must-see for fans of the Marvel Cinematic Universe. With its impressive action sequences, solid performances, and exploration of complex themes, the movie is a game-changer in the superhero genre.  Overall, Iron Man 3 is a brilliant film that offers a unique and thought-provoking perspective on the superhero genre. With its impressive action sequences, solid performances, and exploration of complex themes, the movie is a must-see for fans of the Marvel Cinematic Universe.

The way the narrative addresses various discussions such as the evolution of humanity, representation and diversity, makes the film very different from the most of the films In the film, we follow a group of ten super-powered beings who came to Earth on a specific mission, given by the Celestial Arishem (voice of David Kaye): to end the threat of the Deviant monsters and, in the process, help humanity evolve without get involved in their conflicts. They arrive in the world at the dawn of human civilization and, over time, become immersed in the planet's culture, clinging to and mixing with their protÃ©gÃ©s.  The lack of tone change, rampant fanservice, and the immense need to connect everything in the MCU (Marvel Cinematic Universe) was generating a lot of friction between fans, producers and even movie lovers, raising some important questions, for example: is it still worth following this shared universe? And the movies/series need to be all connected or couldn't they be more independent?  Therefore, Eternals came with the promise of giving a greater freshness to this saturated universe, mainly by counting on ChloÃ© Zhao at the head of the project. The director and screenwriter won two Oscars for Nomadland, for best picture and best director. In fact, Kevin Feige, executive producer at Marvel, allowed Zhao to work with a lot of freedom. Despite still being a 'Marvel product', Eternals already positively differentiates itself by counting on the director's creative vision, which is wide open in all aspects of the film.  For this mixture of astronaut gods, Gnosticism and superheroes to work, Zhao chose to put the brakes on the action so that we could get to know each of the new protagonists in depth, how they relate to each other and how they see the world they live in - the which is essential for us to connect to the story. It just takes patience that the MCU has never demanded from its audience. And the big point is that fights every 10 minutes or that unbridled rush are not needed around here. It's a story about relationships and how that's the most human thing about us-even if it comes from near-divine beings. As much as the slower pace is very different from the formula that Marvel has presented us for almost a decade, the result is one of the most interesting stories in this universe.  The most important thing in Eternals is its characters. What makes the movie truly captivating is the dynamics of Marvel's gods. Setting up an origin story with several protagonists is no easy task, especially when no character is really known to the public. And the great merit of Eternals is to be able to present and develop each one of these immortal beings, dedicating a good part of the script so that we get to know, become affectionate and understand each member of the group, their personality and their way of seeing the world.  With a team of many heroes, Eternals has a large and diverse cast (perhaps the most varied in the history of the genre) and also irregular, which is normal when it comes to different personalities, ages and different genres. This is how we see Sersi's (Gemma Chan) passion for humanity, Kingo's (Kumail Nanjiani) narcissism and the Goblin's (Lia McHugh) frustration with her own nature. Each of the new heroes is portrayed with a unique personality that sets them apart from the rest, and they are all equally interesting and captivating. Everyone has their highlight moment, mixing well the typical Marvel humor with the particularities of each one. They all work well on their own and are even better together. Even Richard Madden's Ikaris is a great character, despite the actor's limited performance.  One of the main challenges of the script is to reconcile the wide range of characters - and the strong cast. Not just highlighting and developing each one, but making them relevant to the story. Does the film succeed? So-so. There are characters with little screen time and others appearing too much. Even more, you could feel the lack of depth in the central characters. Although the film presents interesting contexts and backgrounds for everyone, the personalities follow very simple archetypes, something that creates the impression that these heroes are just "more of the same". However, it doesn't necessarily mean something bad. Overall, the dynamic between the members works well. They have chemistry with each other and are charismatic most of the time.  And those character dynamics are key to making the whole story work. The real tension of the script isn't in how the group will defeat the Deviants or save the world, but how their different ways of looking at humanity and the mission they've been given clash - and for those conflicts to work, the people need to care about all ten eternals, which the movie does very well. But, as said, it takes time for all these relationships to be explored - and that's where Eternals hits the brakes on what we're used to seeing in the MCU. So, instead of having several action sequences that give the plot a celerity and a sense of urgency, the film is dedicated to showing the relationship between Ikaris and Sersi, to justify the mutual admiration of Gilgamesh (Don Lee) and Thena (Angelina Jolie) and explain why Druig (Barry Keoghan) disagrees with his brothers' methods.  The narrative is linear, but it alternates between the present day and important moments in human history, going back and forth in flashbacks. In this way, at the same time that we follow the trajectory of the heroes in getting together, we see what led them to be in this situation. Definitely, the film breaks several standards set by Marvel. It cares a lot more about telling a story than inflating the MCU even more with references and warnings about upcoming movies.  However, the influence of Marvel is still present, unfortunately in a negative way. There are characters needlessly out of tune, which only serve to bring comic relief. Not that it's bad, most of the funny scenes work, but it totally breaks the rhythm and many are no longer taken seriously. Furthermore, there are many inconsistencies that are difficult to swallow, especially involving the reasons why Deviants exist. The impression left at the end is that the alien beings only have the purpose of composing action scenes.  Yes, there are some problems in "Eternals", such as digital effects that are not impressive, some arms of the story that are not that interesting, secondary characters that don't arouse any feelings, a semi-villain poorly explored and, above all, a feeling that that story doesn't take place in the Marvel movie universe. Despite the script having some notable holes, and the film's duration being excessively long, it still delivers a good, engaging story that brings a freshness that Marvel so badly needed. Thanks to ChloÃ© Zhao, Eternals is one of the films with the most personality in this universe. It is remarkable how great the direction of scenes and actors are. The director brings her naturalistic perspective to filming, valuing every detail to the fullest. All the combat choreography is very well done, taking advantage of both the abilities of each of the eternals and the visual impact they offer to compose scenes that are really impactful.  With stunning scenes filmed in real locations, Eternals is a very beautiful film both in terms of looks and in the message of union and love that it conveys to viewers. The soundtrack is noteworthy and completes the epic setting, emphasizing emotional moments and punctuating other scenes to amplify the effect of what is happening on screen. Another peculiar point is that, despite the soberer and contemplative tone than we are used to in the MCU, the long is not afraid to make references either to other Disney franchises or even to characters from the "competitor" DC Comics, responding in the can to fan-asked questions like "Why didn't the Eternals help in the fight against Thanos" and more. It's interesting to see a direct, honest and wide-open answer on the big screen.  In addition, the way the narrative addresses various discussions such as the evolution of humanity, representation and diversity, makes the film very different from more banal productions that lead nowhere - as is the case with several feature films of the genre. Although Eternals still has several slips, it delivers a solid story that works very well for making us care about those illustrious strangers. The whole dynamic of these gods is what sustains the plot and makes the twists and conflicts have the necessary weight both to impact us and to design what is to come in the future of the cinematic universe.  It was a tremendous success to bring someone like ChloÃ© Zhao, who brought identity, body and soul to a project that had everything to go wrong. Even with Marvel intervening to connect the film to its cinematic universe, the director and screenwriter's vision was essential for us to have a good story, without having to get so attached to the MCU. Now, it remains to be seen if Marvel will continue to bring in Zhao and other professionals as good as they do because that's exactly what their films need, directors who bring personality traits to their films. The shadow created by Avengers: Endgame is still a problem for this Phase 4. The public's expectation and anxiety for something as great as a monster that Marvel itself created and that now seems to struggle to tame. However, audiences are sure to find Eternals one of the most interesting things the MCU has offered in a long time.

It was strange This is literally one of the most unusual films in the MCU. Its basic premise is that Wanda is trying to replace herself in a Universe where she has children and Doctor Strange has to stop her. This might be the most action-packed MCU film ever! The pacing was great, and the creativity was wonderful! That being said, it did have problems. It featured the Illuminati who were a real letdown.  It featured a bunch of great characters who were previously unseen (wonderful of Patrick Stewart to reprise his role), but sadly they get killed off pretty quickly. Still, the good outweighs the bad. While the original's a classic, this did some great world building. Maybe it could have used more humor, but certainly a film that should be seen. There are always new ideas being put up against the viewer. I'm glad it wasn't too long or short either. ***

Do you fondue? The movie starts at its WWII origins. Chris Evans plays Steve Rogers a puny 4F man who has lied his way into a military science experiment. Tommy Lee Jones plays an excellent Colonel. Meanwhile evil Nazi Hugo Weaving believing he is Darth Maul, has his own plans for world conquest using sharks with frickin' laser beams. (Just kidding about the sharks.) Rogers is a 90 pound weakling who displays intellect, courage, and guts. He is selected for the military's secret program and is transformed into a muscular super hero. The special effects were very good, although we do know from "Last Action Hero" you can't really jump around car roofs. After Rogers is transformed he becomes Captain America to be used as a recruiting tool, as Tommy Lee still has his doubts about the 90 pound weakling. Jackson from Iron Man 2 which hints at an ultimate sequel providing everyone isn't dead by then.  The movie combines action, drama, light comedy, and light romance.
